content:
Given below are two statements.

Statement-I : Adiabatic work done is positive when work is done on the system and internal energy of the system increases.

Statement - II : No work is done during free expansion of an ideal gas.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.

options.content: Both statements - I and Statement - II are false
Statement - I is true but statement - II is false
Statement - I is false but statement - II is true
Both statements - I and Statement - II are true.

explanation:
Option D

Explanation:

Statement I

For an adiabatic process $Q=0$.

If “work done” $W$ is taken positive when done on the system, then the first law gives

$$ \Delta U \;=\; Q + W \;=\; 0 + W \;=\; W. $$

Thus if work is done on the system $(W>0)$, its internal energy increases $(\Delta U>0)$.

Statement II

In a free (Joule) expansion the external pressure $P_{\rm ext}=0$, so

$$ W \;=\;\int P_{\rm ext}\,dV \;=\;0. $$

Hence no work is done during the free expansion of an ideal gas.

Both statements are therefore true.

content: Which one of the following reactions has $\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{U}$ ?
explanation:
To determine which reaction has $\Delta H = \Delta U$, we use the equation:

$ \Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g \cdot RT $

Where:

$\Delta H$ is the change in enthalpy.

$\Delta U$ is the change in internal energy.

$\Delta n_g$ is the change in the number of moles of gas.

$R$ is the ideal gas constant.

$T$ is the temperature in Kelvin.

For the reaction:

$ 2 \text{HI}_{(\text{g})} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_{2(\text{g})} + \text{I}_{2(\text{g})} $

The change in the number of moles of gas ($\Delta n_g$) can be calculated as follows:

On the reactant side, there are 2 moles of gas.

On the product side, there are 1 mole of $\text{H}_2$ plus 1 mole of $\text{I}_2$, totaling 2 moles.

Thus, $\Delta n_g = 2 - 2 = 0$.

Since $\Delta n_g = 0$, it follows that:

$ \Delta H = \Delta U $

Therefore, for this reaction, the change in enthalpy is equal to the change in internal energy.

content:
Identify the incorrect statements among the following:

(a) All enthalpies of fusion are positive

(b) The magnitude of enthalpy change does not depend on the strength of the intermolecular interactions in the substance undergoing phase transformations.

(c) When a chemical reaction is reversed, the value of $\Delta \mathrm{rH}^{\circ}$ is reversed in sign.

(d) The change in enthalpy is dependent of path between initial state (reactants) and final state (products)

(e) For most of the ionic compounds, $\Delta_{\text {sol }} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ is negative
explanation:
The incorrect statements are (b), (d) and (e), so the correct choice is Option B.

• (b) is false: ΔH for a phase change does depend on how strong the intermolecular forces are—you must supply enough energy to overcome them.

• (d) is false: Enthalpy is a state function, so ΔH depends only on initial and final states, not on the path.

• (e) is false: Many ionic salts dissolve endothermically (ΔsolH° > 0); only some give off heat on dissolving (ΔsolH° < 0).

(a) and (c) are both true.

From the diagram $(Z)=\frac{V_{\text {real }}}{V_{\text {ideal }}}$

$\Delta_r H$ for the reaction, $C \rightarrow A$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\Delta_r H \text { for } C \rightarrow A \text { is given by }\\ &\begin{gathered} \underset{(A \rightarrow C)}{\Delta H_C}=\underset{(A \rightarrow B)}{\Delta H_1}+\underset{(B \rightarrow C)}{\Delta H_2} \\ \underset{(A \rightarrow C)}{\Delta H}=10+25=35 \mathrm{~J} \\ \underset{C \rightarrow A}{\Delta H}=-\underset{A \rightarrow C}{\Delta H} \\ \underset{C \rightarrow A}{\Delta H}=-35 \mathrm{~J} \end{gathered} \end{aligned}$$

content: A gas at a pressure of $$2 \mathrm{~atm}$$ is heated from $$25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ to $$323^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ and simultaneously compressed of $$\frac{2}{3}$$rd of its original value. Then the final pressure is
explanation:
Given, $$p_1=2 \mathrm{~atm}$$

$$\begin{gathered} p_2=? \\ V_1=1 \mathrm{~L} \\ V_2=\frac{3}{2} \\ T_1=298 \mathrm{~K} \\ T_2=596 \mathrm{~K} \end{gathered}$$

Using combined gas law, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{p_1 V_1}{T_1}=\frac{p_2 V_2}{T_2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad p_2 & =\frac{2 \times 1}{298} \times \frac{3 \times 596}{2}=6 \mathrm{~atm} \end{aligned}$$

content: Lattice enthalpy for $$\mathrm{NaCl}$$ is $$+788 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$ and $$\Delta H_{\text {hyd }}^{\circ}=-784 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$. Enthalpy of solution of $$\mathrm{NaCl}$$ is
Temperature of $$25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ in Fahrenheit and Kelvin scale respectively are

The work done when 2 moles of an ideal gas expands rèversibly and isothermally from a volume of $$1 \mathrm{~L}$$ to $$10 \mathrm{~L}$$ at $$300 \mathrm{~K}$$ is ($$R=0.0083 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$$)
explanation:
Given, initial volume $$V_1=1 \mathrm{~L}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Final volume, } V_2=2 \mathrm{~L} \\ & \text { Temperature }=300 \mathrm{~K} \\ & \qquad R=0.0083 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Moles $$(n)=2$$ moles

We know that,

Work done, $$W=2.303 n R T \log \frac{V_2}{V_1}$$

$$=2.303 \times 2 \times 0.0083 \times 300 \log 10=11.5 \mathrm{~kJ}$$

content: When the same quantity of heat is absorbed by a system at two different temperatures $$T_1$$ and $$T_2$$, such that $$T_1>T_2$$, change in entropies are $$\Delta S_1$$ and $$\Delta S_2$$ respectively. Then
explanation:
When the same quantity of heat is absorbed by a system at two different temperatures, $$T_1$$ and $$T_2$$, such that $$T_1 > T_2$$, the change in entropies are $$\Delta S_1$$ and $$\Delta S_2$$ respectively.

Given that entropy change is given by :

$$ \Delta S = \frac{Q}{T} $$

For the same heat absorbed,

$$ \Delta S_1 = \frac{Q}{T_1} $$

$$ \Delta S_2 = \frac{Q}{T_2} $$

Since $$T_1 > T_2$$,

$$ \Delta S_1 < \Delta S_2 $$.

Option A :

$$\Delta S_1 < \Delta S_2$$

content: The reaction in which $$\Delta H=\Delta U$$ is
explanation:
Key Idea Relation between $$\Delta H$$ and $$\Delta U$$ is given as $$\Delta H=\Delta U+\Delta n g R T$$

when $$\Delta n g=0$$, then $$\Delta H=\Delta U$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Delta n g=+v e, \text { then } \Delta H>\Delta U \\ & \Delta n g=-v e, \text { then } \Delta H<\Delta U \end{aligned}$$

The reaction in which $$\Delta H>\Delta U$$ is

$$\mathrm{CaCO}_3(s) \rightarrow \mathrm{CaO}(s)+\mathrm{CO}_2(g)$$

Here, $$\Delta n g=\Sigma n_{\text {produsts }}-\Sigma n_{\text {reactants }}=1-0=1$$

As we know $$\Delta H=\Delta U+\Delta n g R T$$

$$\therefore \quad \Delta H>\Delta U$$

content: A reaction has both $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ -ve. The rate of reaction

options.content: cannot be predicted for change in temperature
remains unaffected by change in temperature

explanation:
In this scenario, both the change in enthalpy ($\Delta H$) and the change in entropy ($\Delta S$) are negative.

To determine the effect of temperature on the reaction, we consider the Gibbs free energy equation:

$ \Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S $

For a reaction to be spontaneous, $\Delta G$ must be negative. Given that both $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ are negative, we want the absolute value of $\Delta H$ to be greater than $T \Delta S$ to ensure $\Delta G$ remains negative:

$ \Delta H > T \Delta S $

This inequality suggests that at lower temperatures, the negative enthalpy term ($\Delta H$) dominates, making it more likely for $\Delta G$ to be negative. Therefore, the reaction is exothermic and more favorable at lower temperatures, increasing its rate as the temperature decreases.
